> Jeff Patton (independent agile/product coach); drew on foundational ideas from Alistair Cockburn's Crystal Clear methodology (walking skeleton concept), Kent Beck's Extreme Programming (user stories), and the Agile Manifesto (2001)


**Core Philosophy**

- **Driving Insight**: 

> A flat, prioritized backlog of user stories loses the narrative — the big picture of what users actually do and why. Teams optimize individual backlog items without understanding how they connect into a coherent user journey. By arranging stories on a 2D map (horizontal = user's chronological narrative flow, vertical = criticality/depth), you restore the story: the backbone shows the end-to-end user journey, the walking skeleton defines the thinnest slice that works end-to-end, and the vertical dimension communicates relative priority and release planning. The core insight is that 'telling stories' (narrative) is fundamentally different from 'writing stories' (backlog items), and the map bridges the two.

- **Decision Style**: 

> deliberative and consensus-driven — the mapping workshop is a collaborative exercise where stakeholders, developers, and users jointly construct the map, negotiate what belongs at each depth level, and reach consensus on the walking skeleton (MVP slice). The map provides a shared visual artifact that anchors prioritization conversations, but the decisions themselves emerge from group deliberation, not an algorithmic formula.

- **Problem Class Targeted**: 

> ill-defined product development problems where the full scope of user needs is unknown or emergent. Targeted at software products with a user-facing interaction flow (web apps, mobile apps, SaaS platforms). Particularly suited to greenfield products and major feature expansions where the team needs to discover the right product, not just build a pre-specified one. Less suited to back-end infrastructure, algorithmic optimization, or compliance-driven projects where user narrative is secondary.


**Phase Structure**

- **Ordered Phases**: 

> (1) Frame: define the product vision, target users, and scope boundary; (2) Map the Backbone: identify the high-level activities (verbs) users perform, arranged left-to-right in chronological narrative order; (3) Map the Walking Skeleton: under each backbone activity, place the minimal set of stories that create an end-to-end working slice — the thinnest vertical slice through the entire narrative; (4) Fill the Body: add finer-grained stories and acceptance criteria under each backbone activity, building depth; (5) Slice Out Releases: draw horizontal slicing lines to define MVP, Release 1, Release 2, etc., each representing a complete narrative flow with increasing depth; (6) Develop a Release Strategy: assign stories to sprints based on the slice lines, always maintaining a working end-to-end product after each slice.

- **Feedback Loops**: 

> Explicit and central: (1) user feedback on each release slice informs reprioritization of deeper stories — the map is continuously refined based on what users actually value; (2) the walking skeleton is verified by walking through it with users (narrative validation); (3) discovery during development (a story proves harder than expected) loops back to the map to adjust slicing or depth placement; (4) the map itself serves as a progress visualization — 'how far down the map are we?' — creating a continuous feedback loop between plan and reality.

- **Gates**:
  

> Primary gate is the narrative walkthrough: after mapping the walking skeleton, the team walks through every backbone activity end-to-end, asking 'can a user complete this journey?' If the walkthrough fails (a gap is found or a step is impossible), the skeleton must be revised. Release planning creates a second gate: stakeholders approve which slice lines define each release. There are no automated gates — all are collaborative human review checkpoints.

- **Exit Criteria**: 

> A user story map is 'done' when: (1) the backbone covers the full user journey for the defined scope; (2) a walking skeleton (thinnest end-to-end slice) is identified; (3) stories at each depth level are estimated and prioritized; (4) release slicing lines are drawn and agreed. Importantly, the map is never 'finished' — it is a living artifact that evolves with the product. Partial states: a backbone-only map is useful for vision communication; a skeleton-only map supports MVP planning.


**Decomposition**

- **Decomposition Method**:
  

> value-driven, narrative-driven, two-dimensional. The primary decomposition dimension is horizontal: the user's chronological narrative flow (activities arranged left-to-right). The secondary decomposition is vertical: depth of functionality, from walking skeleton (must-have, minimal) at the top to nice-to-have enhancements at the bottom. This is fundamentally different from technical decomposition (front-end, back-end, database) — it decomposes by user value delivery rather than by system architecture.

- **Granularity Guidance**: 

> Backbone activities: 7-15 high-level user verbs (e.g., 'browse products', 'place order', 'track shipment'). Walking skeleton stories: 1-3 per backbone activity — just enough to complete the user journey end-to-end. Depth stories: as many as needed to capture all desired functionality (can be 50-200+ total). A story maps to roughly 1-5 days of development work. Recursion stops when stories are granular enough for a single sprint. Jeff Patton emphasizes: 'tell stories, don't write them' — the map structures conversation, not specification.

- **Boundary Rules**: 

> Horizontal boundaries: activities should be distinct user goals — no overlap in the narrative flow (user does X, then Y, then Z). Vertical boundaries: release slicing lines cut horizontally across all backbone activities — every slice delivers a complete user journey, preventing 'siloed' releases where one part of the app is deep but nothing works end-to-end. The fundamental boundary rule is: NEVER ship a release that breaks the narrative — every slice must be a walkable story from start to finish.


**Risk & Uncertainty**

- **Risk Assessment Mechanism**:
  

> Risk is assessed through: (1) narrative completeness — gaps in the backbone are visible as missing activities; (2) skeleton walkthrough — the team identifies technical risk points during the walkthrough (what could break the end-to-end flow?); (3) dependency awareness — backbone ordering reveals upstream/downstream dependencies; (4) scope risk — the vertical depth reveals where the team might over-build before validating the narrative. No formal risk scoring; risk is surfaced through collaborative conversation during mapping workshops.

- **Unknown Handling**:
  

> Unknowns are placed on the map as placeholder stories (often with question marks or rough descriptions) and positioned at an appropriate depth. The map makes unknowns visible rather than hiding them. During skeleton walkthroughs, unknown areas are explicitly flagged — 'we don't know how this step works yet' — and may be prioritized as spikes/investigations before the release slice that depends on them.

- **Default Stance On Unknown**: 

> PERMISSIVE — unknowns are acknowledged and placed on the map but do not block progress on other parts of the narrative. Unknowns are treated as risks to be investigated, not blockers to be resolved before any work begins. The walking skeleton approach explicitly accepts that unknowns will be discovered and resolved iteratively.

- **Safety Guardrails**:
  

> None for operational safety. User Story Mapping is a planning/communication technique. Guardrails are organizational: the collaborative workshop format ensures multiple perspectives surface risks; the narrative walkthrough catches gaps before development starts; the iterative release slicing ensures no single release is catastrophic since each slice is small and correctable.


**Human Role**

- **Human Decision Points**:
  

> Humans are involved at every step: the entire method is a collaborative human activity. Key decision points: (1) scope framing — what users and activities are in/out; (2) backbone ordering — what's the right narrative sequence; (3) walking skeleton selection — which stories are truly minimal; (4) release slicing — where to draw the horizontal lines; (5) re-mapping — when new learning invalidates the current map. The human IS the method — there is no automated component. The human is IN the loop at every decision.

- **Override Capability**: 

> Any participant in the mapping workshop can propose changes to the map at any time. The map is a collaborative artifact with no formal change control — override is by group consensus. Structure can be changed at any granularity: reorder backbone activities, move stories between depth levels, redraw slicing lines.

- **Fallback When No Human**: 

> Not applicable. User Story Mapping requires human collaboration to function. Without humans in a workshop, the map is a static artifact that cannot adapt to new learning. There is no automated equivalent.


**Scalability & Cost**

- **Max Problem Size**: 

> Has been applied to products with 200-500+ user stories spanning multiple teams (10-30+ developers). The practical limit is: the physical/virtual map must be viewable in a single workshop session (typically a wall-sized board or large Miro/Mural canvas). Beyond ~15 backbone activities and ~200 stories, the map becomes visually unwieldy. Large products typically use multiple maps (one per user persona or product area). There is no theoretical limit — the constraint is workshop manageability.

- **Parallel Execution**:
  

> Strongly supports parallel execution: once the backbone and walking skeleton are defined, multiple teams can work in parallel on different backbone activities (e.g., one team on 'browse products', another on 'checkout'). The release slice constraint (every slice is end-to-end) ensures that parallel work converges to a coherent product. Dependencies are resolved by the backbone ordering: a team working on a downstream activity can stub out upstream dependencies while the upstream team is still building. Parallelism is first-class by design — the map visualizes what can be developed concurrently.

- **Team Scaling Model**: 

> Linear to sub-linear. One map typically serves 1-3 development teams (5-20 developers). For larger products, the map is split into multiple sub-maps (one per user persona/product area), each owned by a sub-team. Coordination overhead grows with the number of backbone activities that span teams. The map provides a coordination framework (shared narrative) that reduces the overhead of large-team coordination compared to flat-backlog approaches.

- **Cost Scaling Model**:
  

> Workshop cost: 1-3 days of full-team participation for initial mapping, then ~2-4 hours per sprint for map refinement. There is no computational cost — all cost is human collaboration time. Map maintenance cost scales linearly with the number of stories being tracked. The primary value proposition is cost avoidance: the map prevents building the wrong product, which is far more expensive than the mapping overhead.


**Adaptation & Recovery**

- **Replanning Built In**:
  

> Replanning is first-class and continuous. The map is explicitly designed to be re-sliced and re-prioritized as learning accumulates. Replanning is local by default: you adjust the affected area of the map (a specific backbone activity or depth level) rather than rebuilding the entire map. However, major pivots (a fundamentally different user narrative) require global remapping. The map's visual nature makes replanning low-friction — you physically move cards on the map.

- **Trigger Mechanism**: 

> Triggers: (1) user feedback invalidates an assumption about the narrative flow; (2) development reveals a story is harder/easier than estimated, requiring depth rebalancing; (3) new user research reveals a missing backbone activity; (4) stakeholder strategy shift changes release priorities; (5) the walking skeleton walkthrough reveals a fatal gap. All triggers are human-recognized and human-driven.

- **Adaptation Cost**: 

> Local adaptation (reordering stories within a backbone activity, adjusting a single release slice) is very low cost — minutes in a sprint planning meeting. Adding a new backbone activity is moderate cost — requires reassessing the narrative flow for adjacent activities and their story depth. Global remapping is high cost — days of workshop time — but is rare and only triggered by major strategic pivots.

- **Failure Recovery Mechanism**:
  

> Failure modes: (1) a release slice proves incomplete — the narrative walkthrough catches this before the release ships, and the slice is adjusted; (2) a backbone activity proves irrelevant — it is removed from the map and its stories are either discarded or redistributed; (3) 'map rot' — the map is not maintained and becomes disconnected from reality — recovery requires a fresh mapping workshop. Blast radius is contained by the slice structure: a bad release affects only one slice, not the entire product. The map's visual nature makes failure visible — gaps and orphans are literally visible as empty or disconnected areas on the board.


**Communication**

- **Output Artifacts**: 

> The map itself (physical wall of cards or digital board in Miro/Mural/Jira), release plan (slicing lines annotated with target dates), walking skeleton definition (which stories are in MVP), sprint backlogs derived from the map, user journey narrative (the backbone of activities as a story). The map IS the primary artifact — all other artifacts are views or subsets of the map.

- **Briefing Format**: 

> The map IS the briefing format — a 2D visual with backbone activities across the top and depth stories below. For executive communication: the walking skeleton (MVP definition) with narrative walkthrough demonstration; the release plan showing which slices deliver when; a progress overlay showing 'we are here' on the map. Jeff Patton recommends telling the user's story by physically walking through the map — pointing at each backbone activity and narrating 'first the user does X, then Y, then Z.'

- **Traceability**: 

> Strong narrative traceability: every story traces to a backbone activity (why are we building this — the user's goal) and a release slice (when will it ship). The map preserves the rationale for priority (higher on the map = more critical to the narrative). However, traceability to original assumptions (why is THIS the backbone order?) is typically captured in workshop notes, not in the map artifact itself. Decision rationale degrades over time if not explicitly documented.

- **Agent Communication Protocol**:
  

> Not applicable. The map is a human collaboration artifact. In a multi-agent context, the map structure (backbone activities, story depth levels, release slices) could be serialized as structured YAML/JSON and communicated to agents as a task decomposition with temporal ordering constraints. However, the rich collaborative meaning-making of a mapping workshop does not translate to agent communication.


**Learning & Memory**

- **Learning Memory Architecture**:
  

> Implicit organizational learning: over successive releases, the team builds intuition about realistic walking skeleton scope, appropriate depth levels, and effective slicing patterns. The map itself serves as an organizational memory artifact — the history of what was built, when, and why. Post-release retrospectives capture lessons about the mapping process. No formal learning mechanism; the primary memory is the physical/digital map artifact and team experience.


**Performance**

- **Latency Bounds**: 

> Interactive (workshop sessions produce a usable map in hours to days). The initial mapping workshop takes 1-3 days. Sprint-level map refinement takes 1-2 hours. Under time pressure, a 'quick map' (backbone + skeleton only, skipping depth details) can be produced in 2-4 hours. The method degrades gracefully — even a backbone-only map provides more narrative context than a flat backlog.

- **Evaluation Benchmarking**:
  

> No formal benchmarks exist. Validation is qualitative and project-specific: do teams using story maps deliver products that better match user needs? Evidence is anecdotal and practitioner-driven. Jeff Patton's book includes case studies from multiple companies (no controlled experiments). Adoption by major agile organizations (Spotify, SAP, Salesforce teams) serves as indirect validation. Some surveys report improved stakeholder alignment and reduced rework, but rigorous empirical studies are limited.


**SW/LLM Applicability**

- **Mapping Strength**:
  

> Strong mapping to LLM agent task decomposition: the narrative backbone maps to the user/agent journey — what does the agent need to accomplish, in what chronological order? The walking skeleton maps to the minimal viable agent pipeline (end-to-end but threadbare). Vertical depth maps to agent capability enhancement — adding sophistication to each step (better prompts, more tools, richer feedback). Release slicing maps to incremental agent deployment. The method's emphasis on user-value-driven decomposition (rather than technical decomposition) is highly relevant to designing agent workflows that serve human goals.

- **Gaps**:
  

> Key gaps for software/agent contexts: (1) the method assumes human-readable user narratives, but agent workflows may have no visible 'user journey' — the 'user' may be another system; (2) no support for non-narrative task structures — many agent tasks are tree-shaped (exploratory) or graph-shaped (interdependent), not linear narratives; (3) the method's strength is in collaborative human workshops, which don't translate to autonomous agent planning; (4) no formal dependency representation beyond left-to-right ordering — cross-backbone dependencies are handled by conversation, not systematic analysis; (5) the map has no notion of feedback loops or iteration within a single backbone activity.

- **Adaptation Required**:
  

> For JORDAN: (1) Adapt the backbone concept to represent the agent's 'mission phases' rather than a user's activities — e.g., 'understand task, research, decompose, execute, verify, report'; (2) replace the workshop format with LLM-generated backbones and skeletons, verified by human review; (3) add explicit dependency edges between backbone activities to complement the left-to-right narrative with formal dependency modeling (DSM/CPM integration); (4) use vertical depth to represent agent capability tiers — Level 1: basic prompt, Level 2: with tool access, Level 3: with verification loop; (5) map release slices to JORDAN pipeline stages, where each slice represents a complete end-to-end agent capability delivered incrementally.

- **Analogous Jordan Node**: plan

**Empirical Record**

- **Validation Context**: 

> Validated through practitioner adoption in agile software development since 2005. Used by product teams at companies including Spotify, SAP, Lego, and numerous agile consultancies. Jeff Patton's book (2014) has sold over 100,000 copies and is standard reading in agile product management certification (CSPO). Validation is primarily anecdotal and community-driven — no controlled experiments or academic studies with statistical rigor. The method has been presented at major agile conferences (Agile 20XX, XP Conference) and is integrated into SAFe (Scaled Agile Framework) as 'Story Mapping' in the Program Increment planning process.

- **Known Failures**:
  

> Documented failures: (1) 'flat map syndrome' — teams treat the map as a 2D backlog and continue to optimize individual stories without using the narrative context; (2) backbone bloat — too many backbone activities make the map unreadable and defeats the narrative purpose; (3) skeleton 'death march' — the walking skeleton is the right concept but is often too ambitious, leading to months-long MVPs that aren't minimal; (4) map abandonment — the map is created in a workshop with enthusiasm but never updated, becoming disconnected from the actual sprint backlog within 2-3 sprints; (5) narrative fiction — the backbone assumes a linear user journey when users actually navigate non-linearly, making the map misleading; (6) executive misinterpretation — stakeholders see a full map and assume all stories will be built, missing the slicing concept. The method also fails when applied to purely internal/backend work where no coherent user narrative exists.

- **Where Still Used**: 

> Actively used in: agile product management (SaaS companies, enterprise IT, digital agencies), UX design practices, SAFe Program Increment planning, and product discovery workshops. Tools with explicit story mapping support: Jira (via plugins like Easy Agile User Story Maps), Miro (template), Mural, StoriesOnBoard, Avion. Adopted as a core practice in agile coaching and CSPO (Certified Scrum Product Owner) training. Jeff Patton continues to teach and consult on story mapping globally.


---

### 58. Voyager (Wang et al., 2023) / DEPS (Wang et al., 2023) — Lifelong Learning Agents with Skill Libraries and Interactive Planning {#voyager-wang-et-al-2023-deps-wang-et-al-2023-lifelong-learning-agents-with-skill-libraries-and-interactive-planning}

**Basic Identity**

- **Name**: 

> Voyager (Wang et al., 2023) / DEPS (Wang et al., 2023) — Lifelong Learning Agents with Skill Libraries and Interactive Planning

- **Origin Domain**: 

> AI research (embodied agents, open-world game playing, lifelong learning, large language model agents)

- **Year Developed**: 

> Both 2023. DEPS (Describe, Explain, Plan and Select): arXiv:2302.01560, February 2023; accepted at NeurIPS 2023. Voyager: arXiv:2305.16291, May 2023. Both papers use MineDojo as the Minecraft environment and GPT-4 as the reasoning backbone, but address complementary challenges: DEPS focuses on robust multi-step planning with error correction, while Voyager focuses on accumulating reusable skills over a lifetime of play.

- **Progenitor**: 

> Voyager: Guanzhi Wang, Yuqi Xie, Yunfan Jiang, Ajay Mandlekar, Chaowei Xiao, Yuke Zhu, Linxi Fan, Anima Anandkumar (NVIDIA, Caltech, Stanford, UT Austin). Linxi Fan (Jim Fan) and Anima Anandkumar are senior authors — both prominent in embodied AI and LLM agent research at NVIDIA. DEPS: Zihao Wang, Shaofei Cai, Guanzhou Chen, Anji Liu, Xiaojian Ma, Yitao Liang (Peking University, UCLA, PKU-BAAI). Note: Zihao Wang (DEPS) and Guanzhi Wang (Voyager) are different researchers despite the shared surname.


**Core Philosophy**

- **Driving Insight**: 

> Two intertwined insights. DEPS insight: LLM agents fail not because they can't plan but because their plans don't survive contact with reality. The solution is a tight closed loop where execution feedback (Describe) is analyzed for root causes (Explain), used to revise the plan (Plan), and the most feasible sub-goals are prioritized (Select). This creates an error-driven planning cycle where the LLM learns from its mistakes within a single task. Voyager insight: LLM agents that solve each task from scratch are inefficient — humans accumulate skills over a lifetime and compose them to tackle novel challenges. The solution is a persistent skill library of executable code (JavaScript for Minecraft), grown automatically through an iterative refinement loop, indexed by embedding for retrieval, and composed to form complex behaviors. The combined insight is that capable LLM agents need BOTH interactive error correction (DEPS) AND cumulative skill acquisition (Voyager) — JORDAN has neither: its replanning is primitive (no error root-cause analysis) and its memory is amnesiac (no skill accumulation across missions).

- **Decision Style**: 

> DEPS: hierarchical and error-driven — the Descriptor passively summarizes state, the Explainer (LLM) diagnoses errors, the Planner (LLM) generates revised plans, and the Goal Selector (trained model) chooses the most feasible sub-goal. Decisions flow: goal selection (which sub-goal to pursue) -> plan generation (how to achieve it) -> execution -> failure diagnosis -> replan. The Goal Selector is a trained neural network making probabilistic decisions based on estimated steps-to-completion; the Planner is an LLM making deliberative decisions about action sequences. Voyager: generative and library-driven — the agent faces a situation, retrieves relevant skills from the skill library (embedding-based similarity search), and either: (a) composes retrieved skills to solve the task, or (b) generates new code via GPT-4's iterative prompting mechanism (generate -> execute -> self-verify -> refine -> commit to library). The decision is: do I have a skill for this? If yes, retrieve and compose. If no, generate and learn. This is a hybrid decision architecture blending retrieval (algorithmic) with generation (deliberative LLM). Both approaches are more decision-rich than ReAct because they have explicit failure analysis and skill selection mechanisms.

- **Problem Class Targeted**: 

> Open-world, multi-task environments with large action spaces where: (a) tasks vary widely (crafting, exploration, combat, building in Minecraft); (b) the environment is only partially observable and dynamically changing; (c) skills learned for one task transfer to others (skill composition); (d) the agent must operate autonomously for extended periods (lifelong learning); (e) no single plan works for all tasks — the agent must continuously adapt. DEPS targets the planning robustness problem: how to maintain plan coherence across complex, multi-step tasks in dynamic environments. Voyager targets the skill accumulation problem: how to build a growing repertoire of capabilities that compounds over time. Both target Minecraft as the canonical open-world benchmark — a complex 3D sandbox with a tech tree (wood -> stone -> iron -> diamond), crafting recipes, exploration, and combat. Less suited to: narrow, well-defined tasks where skill transfer is unnecessary; text-only tasks where code-based skills are irrelevant; tasks where the environment provides no feedback for error diagnosis.


**Phase Structure**

- **Ordered Phases**: 

> DEPS operates as a continuous four-phase loop: (1) DESCRIBE — a Descriptor module summarizes the current execution state (agent inventory, environment observations, task progress) as structured text sent to the LLM planner. (2) EXPLAIN — the Explainer (the LLM, prompted to self-diagnose) receives the description and the failed plan, and locates the specific errors: which step failed, why, and what the root cause is. This is error attribution, not just error detection. (3) PLAN — the Planner (LLM) receives the description and explanation and generates a revised plan — a sequence of sub-goals and actions. (4) SELECT — a trainable Goal Selector module ranks parallel candidate sub-goals by estimated steps-to-completion, selecting the most feasible one based on the agent's current state and proximity. The loop continues until the task succeeds or a budget is exhausted. Voyager operates as a three-component system running continuously: (1) Automatic Curriculum — GPT-4 proposes progressively harder tasks based on the agent's current skill level and world state, maximizing exploration. (2) Skill Library — an ever-growing store of executable JavaScript code representing learned behaviors, indexed by embedding for retrieval. (3) Iterative Prompting Mechanism — for each task, the agent generates code (JavaScript), executes it in Minecraft, observes feedback and errors, self-verifies the outcome, and refines the code until successful, then commits the working code to the skill library.

- **Feedback Loops**: 

> DEPS has a tight, explicit error-feedback loop: execution results are Described, failures are Explained (root-cause analysis), plans are revised (Plan), and feasibility is assessed (Select). This is a four-stage feedback loop that converts execution feedback into improved plans. The Explain phase is the critical feedback processing step — it transforms raw observations into diagnostic insights. Voyager has multiple feedback loops: (1) the iterative code refinement loop — generate code, execute in Minecraft, observe errors (syntax errors, runtime failures, task verification failure), and refine — this is a tight generate-execute-debug loop; (2) the curriculum learning loop — task completions update the agent's skill level, which triggers GPT-4 to propose harder tasks, creating a progression feedback loop; (3) the skill library retrieval loop — when facing a new task, the agent retrieves similar skills from the library, which feeds into code generation, creating a knowledge-reuse feedback loop. Both approaches have more sophisticated feedback mechanisms than ReAct (no root-cause analysis, no skill reuse) and P&E (no execution-time refinement).

- **Gates**:
  

> DEPS: the Goal Selector acts as a gate — it ranks candidate sub-goals and selects the most feasible. If no sub-goal is deemed feasible (low feasibility scores across all candidates), the task may be deemed unsolvable from the current state. The Explain phase also serves as a gate — if no error can be diagnosed, replanning is less informed (and more likely to fail). Voyager: the self-verification step in the iterative prompting mechanism acts as a quality gate — code is only committed to the skill library when it has been verified as correct (task completed). This ensures the skill library maintains high quality. Additionally, the environment itself gates progress: Minecraft's physics and mechanics enforce validity (you can't craft a diamond pickaxe without first obtaining diamonds). The gates are more substantive than ReAct's implicit stopping but less formal than MDMP's approval checkpoints.

- **Exit Criteria**: 

> DEPS: a task is complete when the Descriptor reports that the goal state has been reached (e.g., the agent possesses a diamond). A task is failed when: (a) the plan-explanation loop exhausts its budget without reaching the goal; (b) the Goal Selector determines no feasible sub-goal exists; (c) the task times out. Voyager: a skill is learned (committed to the library) when the iterative prompting loop verifies that the generated code successfully completes the task. The curriculum continues indefinitely (lifelong learning) — there is no 'done' state. Exit criteria for individual tasks: the code executes and achieves the task goal, confirmed by self-verification. Both approaches handle partial completion: even if the final goal is not reached, the agent has acquired partial skills (Voyager) or partial progress (DEPS) that reduce the effort for the next attempt.


**Decomposition**

- **Decomposition Method**:
  

> DEPS decomposes tasks through LLM-based planning — the Planner generates sequences of sub-goals that collectively achieve the overall task. This is hierarchical decomposition: the overall task (obtain diamond) decomposes into sub-goals (obtain wood -> craft wooden pickaxe -> mine stone -> craft stone pickaxe -> mine iron -> ...). The Goal Selector further decomposes the planning horizon by selecting which sub-goal to pursue NOW (short-term vs. long-term sub-goal selection). This disentangles global planning (LLM) from local feasibility assessment (trained Goal Selector). Voyager's decomposition is emergent through the skill library and curriculum: (a) the curriculum proposes incrementally harder tasks that decompose the overall tech tree into manageable steps; (b) the skill library naturally decomposes complex behaviors into composable code functions — a complex skill like 'craft a bed' might compose simpler skills like 'punch tree,' 'craft planks,' and 'craft wool.' The decomposition is bottom-up (skills compose into capabilities) rather than top-down (planning breaks tasks into sub-goals). The hybrid approach — top-down planning (DEPS) + bottom-up skill composition (Voyager) — is the most powerful combination.

- **Granularity Guidance**: 

> DEPS: a sub-goal is an achievable unit that can be completed with a single plan (sequence of primitive actions). The granularity is determined by: (a) what the Planner can reliably plan for (a sequence of 3-10 primitive actions); (b) what the Goal Selector can reliably assess feasibility for; (c) what the Minecraft environment exposes as state transitions. Voyager: a skill is an executable JavaScript function that achieves one concrete task (e.g., 'craftWoodenPickaxe()', 'mineStoneBlock()', 'exploreCave()'). The granularity is determined by: (a) what can be encoded as a self-contained code function; (b) what tasks GPT-4's curriculum proposes; (c) what the agent's current capability level can attempt. Skills can vary widely in complexity — from simple ('punch log') to complex ('build shelter'). The skill library naturally grows skills at varying granularity levels, with complex skills composing simpler ones.

- **Boundary Rules**: 

> DEPS: sub-goals have natural boundaries defined by the Minecraft tech tree — prerequisites are enforced by the game (you must have a wooden pickaxe to mine stone). The Describe phase captures the current state, and the Planner uses this to ensure sub-goal preconditions are met before attempting them. The Goal Selector further enforces feasibility boundaries by assessing whether the agent is close to achieving a sub-goal. Voyager: skills have well-defined boundaries — each skill is a self-contained JavaScript function with clear inputs (world state, agent state) and outputs (action executed, world state changed). Skills communicate through the Minecraft world state — one skill changes the world, and the next skill observes the changed world via the environment API. The skill library enforces code-level isolation — skills do not share global state or have side effects on each other. However, skills can have hidden dependencies (skill A assumes skill B already ran) that are not explicitly tracked — the agent handles this through retrieval and composition rather than formal dependency management.


**Risk & Uncertainty**

- **Risk Assessment Mechanism**:
  

> DEPS: the Explain phase serves as an implicit risk diagnosis — it identifies what went wrong, which informs the risk of similar approaches failing again. The Goal Selector's feasibility scoring is a form of risk assessment — a low feasibility score indicates high risk of failure. However, there is no explicit risk methodology — no probabilistic scoring, no risk classification, no risk register. Voyager: the self-verification step in the iterative prompting mechanism is a risk mitigation mechanism — code is verified before being committed to the skill library, reducing the risk of storing faulty skills. The curriculum's task progression (easier -> harder) mitigates the risk of the agent attempting tasks it cannot complete, which would waste resources and potentially corrupt the world state. Both approaches manage risk implicitly through their design rather than through explicit risk assessment mechanisms.

- **Unknown Handling**:
  

> DEPS handles unknowns through its error-driven planning loop: when the plan fails due to unexpected circumstances, the Explain phase analyzes what went wrong, and the Planner revises the plan to account for the new understanding. This is 'fail, analyze, and adapt' — the agent probes the unknown through action, and when the action fails, it learns from the failure. Voyager handles unknowns through code generation and refinement: when the agent encounters a novel task, it generates code, executes it, observes the results (success or specific error), and refines the code based on error feedback. This is iterative trial-and-error learning — the unknown is resolved through experimentation. Both approaches are 'probe and learn' rather than 'resolve before acting.' The key difference from ReAct is the depth of analysis: ReAct just tries something else; DEPS diagnoses WHY it failed; Voyager stores the successful solution for future reuse.

- **Default Stance On Unknown**: 

> PERMISSIVE — both approaches act, observe the results, and adapt based on feedback. This is the core operating model for embodied, open-world agents — you cannot know the world state perfectly, so you must act to learn. DEPS's permissiveness is moderated by the Explain->Plan->Select loop, which prevents blindly repeating failed strategies. Voyager's permissiveness is moderated by the iterative refinement loop (code is refined until it works) and the skill library (once learned, the correct approach is reused). Both approaches are well-suited to their domain (Minecraft) where actions are reversible (you can always gather more wood) and failures are non-catastrophic. For domains with irreversible harmful actions, the permissive stance would need additional safeguards.

- **Safety Guardrails**:
  

> Minecraft provides inherent safety: actions in the game are non-catastrophic in the real world — the worst outcome is a dead agent in a game world. The primary guardrails are: (a) the Minecraft environment bounds the action space (you can only do what Minecraft allows); (b) the code execution is sandboxed within the MineDojo environment; (c) in Voyager, the self-verification step prevents incorrect code from being committed to the skill library; (d) the iterative prompting mechanism has a maximum iteration count preventing infinite refinement loops. For non-game applications: the concepts (skill library, iterative refinement, error-driven planning) transfer, but the safety properties do not — a skill library of executable code for real-world systems would need sandboxing, allowlisting, and human review before deployment.


**Human Role**

- **Human Decision Points**:
  

> Both approaches are fully autonomous — no human-in-the-loop is designed into the systems. The agent plays Minecraft without human intervention. Potential human insertion points: (a) task specification — a human provides the high-level goal (though Voyager's curriculum is automatic); (b) skill library curation — a human reviews and approves skills before they are added to the library in safety-critical domains; (c) plan review — a human reviews the Planner's proposed sub-goal sequence before execution; (d) anomaly intervention — if the agent's behavior becomes pathological (stuck in a loop, destroying progress), a human intervenes. These are extensions beyond the published papers.

- **Override Capability**: 

> No built-in override mechanism. In principle: (a) the agent loop can be paused at any point between actions; (b) the skill library can be edited — a human can add, modify, or remove skills; (c) the Planner's sub-goal sequence can be replaced; (d) the Goal Selector's selection can be overridden. The structured components (skill library, plan sequence) make override practical — a human can inspect the agent's current state and modify specific elements.

- **Fallback When No Human**: 

> Both approaches are designed for fully autonomous, extended operation — Voyager's lifelong learning paradigm implies days or weeks of autonomous play. When no human is available, the agent continues operating autonomously. If the agent gets stuck, it may: (a) exhaust its planning budget and report failure; (b) get stuck in a loop (generate code -> fail -> generate similar code -> fail); (c) in Voyager, the curriculum may propose tasks that are too hard, and the agent fails repeatedly. The systems have no escalation mechanism — they continue trying until budgets are exhausted. For extended autonomous operation, detecting pathological loops and triggering a strategy reset would improve robustness.


**Scalability & Cost**

- **Max Problem Size**: 

> DEPS demonstrated completion of 70+ diverse Minecraft tasks, including the ObtainDiamond grand challenge (first planning-based agent to achieve this). Tasks range from simple (collect wood) to complex (craft diamond pickaxe, requiring navigating the full tech tree with dozens of intermediate steps). Voyager demonstrated: 3.3x more unique items collected vs. baselines, 2.3x longer distances traveled, 15.3x faster tech tree milestone unlocking compared to prior SOTA. The Voyager skill library grows to hundreds of skills over extended play. The theoretical limit is determined by: (a) the Minecraft tech tree depth — the longest crafting chain is ~20 steps; (b) the skill library size — as skills accumulate, retrieval quality may degrade (more skills = more retrieval noise); (c) the GPT-4 context window — the iterative prompting mechanism processes code, error messages, and environment state, bounded by context length. What breaks at scale: (1) the skill library retrieval may return irrelevant skills as the library grows (dilution); (2) the curriculum may propose tasks that are too hard or too easy, reducing learning efficiency; (3) very long crafting chains may exceed the Planner's planning horizon (though DEPS's sub-goal selection mitigates this).

- **Parallel Execution**:
  

> No built-in parallel execution — the agent acts sequentially (one action at a time in Minecraft). However, parallelism concepts are applicable: (a) multiple independent skill candidates can be generated and evaluated in parallel during the iterative prompting mechanism; (b) multiple sub-goals can be evaluated by the Goal Selector in parallel (the paper likely does this but doesn't emphasize it); (c) in principle, multiple Voyager agents could explore different Minecraft worlds in parallel and share a merged skill library. Parallelism is not a first-class design concern because Minecraft is an inherently sequential environment (you can only do one thing at a time, and actions build on each other).

- **Team Scaling Model**: 

> Not addressed — both are single-agent systems. Multi-agent extensions: (a) multiple Voyager agents sharing a merged skill library — Agent A learns 'mine diamonds efficiently,' Agent B learns 'build nether portal,' and both skills are available to all agents; (b) DEPS with role-specialized agents — one agent Describes, another Explains, another Plans, another Selects (though the overhead of inter-agent communication would likely outweigh benefits for a single Minecraft agent); (c) competitive or cooperative multi-agent Minecraft — agents with different skill libraries collaborating or competing. Team scaling with a shared skill library is the most promising extension: each agent contributes to a collective knowledge base, and the group learns faster than any individual. Scaling is super-linear initially (shared skills benefit all agents) but sub-linear as the library saturates (new agents add fewer unique skills).

- **Cost Scaling Model**:
  

> Both approaches are GPT-4-based and incur significant per-task costs. DEPS cost: cost = O(plan_iterations * (Describe + Explain + Plan + Select_calls)). Each iteration requires multiple GPT-4 calls, and complex tasks may require 5-20 iterations. The cost is high — GPT-4 inference for complex Minecraft tasks with many replanning cycles is expensive. Voyager cost: cost = O(curriculum_tasks * (skill_retrieval + code_generation_iterations * (code_gen + execution + verification))). The skill library reduces cost over time — as skills accumulate, known tasks are solved by retrieving and composing existing skills (cheap) rather than generating new code (expensive). This creates a favorable cost trajectory: early learning is expensive (many code generation iterations), but later tasks are cheap (skill reuse). The branching factor is determined by: (a) DEPS's Goal Selector ranking N candidate sub-goals (N is typically small, 3-10); (b) Voyager's code generation attempts per task (typically 1-5 iterations). Cost is approximately linear in task complexity for DEPS and improves with library maturity for Voyager. Neither paper provides detailed cost analysis or comparisons with cheaper models.


**Adaptation & Recovery**

- **Replanning Built In**:
  

> DEPS: replanning is the central mechanism — the entire Describe-Explain-Plan-Select loop IS a replanning system. Replanning is first-class, explicit, and designed — it IS the system, not an add-on. Every iteration is a replan based on execution feedback and error diagnosis. The Explain phase provides root-cause analysis, making replanning informed rather than blind. Voyager: replanning is built into the iterative prompting mechanism — when generated code fails, the agent refines it based on error feedback. This is per-skill replanning (refining the code for a specific task) rather than global replanning (revising the overall strategy). The curriculum also replans at the meta-level — if the agent struggles with tasks at one difficulty level, the curriculum adjusts. Both approaches have more sophisticated replanning than ReAct and P&E: DEPS adds root-cause analysis; Voyager adds persistent learning from replanning (working code is saved).

- **Trigger Mechanism**: 

> DEPS: replanning is triggered by plan failure — the Descriptor detects that the current plan step cannot be executed or the goal state is not reached after plan execution. The trigger is environmental: the Minecraft world state does not match the expected state. Additional triggers: (a) timeout — a plan step takes too long; (b) impossibility detection — the Explainer determines that the current plan cannot succeed from the current state. Voyager: code refinement is triggered by execution failure — the JavaScript code produces an error, or the self-verification step determines the task was not completed. The trigger is programmatic (code fails, verification fails). The curriculum triggers new tasks when the agent has mastered the current difficulty level (skill level increases). Both systems have explicit, designed triggers that are more structured than ReAct's implicit triggers.

- **Adaptation Cost**: 

> DEPS: replanning cost = one full loop iteration (Describe + Explain + Plan + Select). Each iteration is expensive — multiple GPT-4 calls. For complex tasks requiring 10 replanning cycles, the total cost can be 10x the cost of a single successful plan. State preservation: the Minecraft world state is preserved across replanning iterations (the agent's progress is not lost), and the Explain phase preserves diagnostic insights. Voyager: code refinement cost = additional code generation + execution + verification iterations. Each iteration costs one GPT-4 call plus Minecraft execution time. The skill library mitigates long-term adaptation cost — once a skill is learned, it is reused with near-zero cost. The key economic insight: Voyager's adaptation cost is front-loaded (learning skills is expensive) but pays off over the agent's lifetime (reusing skills is cheap). This is analogous to human learning: education is expensive but enables cheap skilled performance.

- **Failure Recovery Mechanism**:
  

> DEPS: three-level recovery. (1) Plan-level recovery — the Explain phase diagnoses the failure, and the Planner revises the plan. The revised plan may use alternative sub-goal sequences to work around the failure. (2) Sub-goal-level recovery — the Goal Selector switches to a different sub-goal if the current one becomes infeasible. (3) Primitive action recovery — individual actions can be retried (e.g., if mining a block fails because the agent moved too far, the agent approaches and tries again). Voyager: two-level recovery. (1) Code-level recovery — the iterative prompting mechanism refines code based on error messages. This handles syntax errors, logic errors, and task verification failures. (2) Skill-level recovery — if a retrieved skill fails, the agent falls back to generating new code (de novo generation). The skill library provides resilience — if one approach fails, the agent has a library of alternatives. Blast radius containment: (a) in DEPS, a failed sub-goal does not invalidate progress on other sub-goals (the Minecraft world state preserves all progress); (b) in Voyager, a failed code generation attempt does not corrupt the skill library (only verified code is committed).


**Communication**

- **Output Artifacts**: 

> DEPS produces: (a) the plan sequence — the chain of sub-goals and actions that achieved the task; (b) the explanation trace — for each failure, the Explainer's diagnosis of what went wrong; (c) the execution trace — the sequence of environment states, actions, and outcomes. Voyager produces: (a) the Skill Library — a collection of executable JavaScript functions, each indexed by description embedding, representing the agent's accumulated capabilities; (b) the Curriculum Trace — the sequence of tasks the curriculum proposed and the agent attempted, with outcomes; (c) the Code Refinement Trace — for each skill, the sequence of code versions from initial generation to working solution. The Skill Library is Voyager's most unique and valuable artifact — it is a portable, growing repository of capability that survives individual task sessions. This is the artifact JORDAN most needs but currently lacks: a persistent, retrievable library of learned patterns and solutions.

- **Briefing Format**: 

> For DEPS: the briefing format is the plan-execution trace showing the sub-goal sequence, which sub-goals succeeded, which failed, what the Explainer diagnosed, and how the Planner adapted. This reads like a troubleshooting log: 'Attempted sub-goal X -> failed due to Y -> diagnosed as Z -> revised plan to W -> succeeded.' For Voyager: the briefing format is the skill library catalog and the curriculum progress report. The skill library is browsable by embedding similarity — skills can be inspected by description. The curriculum trace shows learning progress over time. Both produce more structured and informative briefings than ReAct's prose trajectory — the structured components (plan sequence, skill library, explanation trace) are inherently more analyzable.

- **Traceability**: 

> Strong traceability for the planning process: every plan revision in DEPS is traceable to a specific execution failure and a specific explanation. The causal chain is: action X failed -> explainer said reason Y -> planner revised to Z. This is stronger traceability than ReAct (where the reason for action changes is implicit) or P&E (where replanning rationale is not systematically captured). Voyager: every skill in the library is traceable to: the curriculum task that prompted its creation, the initial code generation, the error feedback that drove refinement, and the final verified code. The skill's provenance is fully documented. Both approaches provide decision trails that support post-hoc analysis, debugging, and knowledge transfer.

- **Agent Communication Protocol**:
  

> Not applicable — both are single-agent systems. In multi-agent extensions: (a) multiple Voyager agents could share a skill library via a central repository, communicating through skill upload/download; (b) DEPS roles (Descriptor, Explainer, Planner, Selector) could be separate agents communicating through structured messages; (c) the Minecraft world state serves as the shared medium — agents communicate indirectly through changes to the shared world. The communication pattern is shared-state rather than message-passing. A standardized protocol (MCP for skill library access, ACP for inter-agent coordination) would enable multi-agent extensions.


**Learning & Memory**

- **Learning Memory Architecture**:
  

> Both approaches have sophisticated learning/memory architectures — this is their primary contribution over prior work. DEPS: (a) the Explain phase serves as an episodic memory of recent failures — the agent remembers what went wrong and why within a task episode; (b) the Goal Selector is a trained model that accumulates experience across tasks — it learns which sub-goals are feasible from which states through training. However, DEPS does not have CROSS-task memory — each task starts fresh. Voyager: (a) the Skill Library is a persistent, retrievable procedural memory — executable code representing learned behaviors; (b) skills are indexed by embedding, enabling similarity-based retrieval; (c) complex skills compose simpler skills, creating a hierarchical skill taxonomy; (d) the skill library GROWS over time and persists across sessions — this is genuine lifelong learning; (e) the curriculum adapts based on the agent's capability level, which implicitly encodes the agent's learning history. Voyager's skill library is the closest existing analog to what JORDAN needs — a persistent, queryable store of proven solution patterns. JORDAN's current architecture is 'amnesiac' — each mission starts from scratch with no benefit from past experience. Voyager demonstrates that persistent skill accumulation is feasible and dramatically improves performance.


**Performance**

- **Latency Bounds**: 

> Not suitable for hard real-time — both approaches involve multiple GPT-4 calls per task, each taking seconds, plus Minecraft execution time (simulation ticks). Typical latency: a simple Minecraft task might take 1-3 GPT-4 calls (5-15 seconds) plus Minecraft execution (seconds to minutes depending on the task). A complex task like ObtainDiamond could take 50+ GPT-4 calls (3-10 minutes of inference) and extended Minecraft play (10-30 minutes). Under time pressure: (a) reduce planning iterations; (b) skip Explanation and replan directly; (c) use cached skills (Voyager) instead of generating new code; (d) reduce the curriculum exploration scope. The approaches are designed for batch/background operation (lifelong learning) rather than interactive use. For JORDAN, the latency is acceptable for complex multi-hour missions but not for sub-second agent interactions.

- **Evaluation Benchmarking**:
  

> DEPS evaluated on: (1) Minecraft — 70+ tasks ranging from simple (collect log) to grand challenge (ObtainDiamond). DEPS was the first planning-based LLM agent to achieve ObtainDiamond. Metrics: task success rate, step efficiency. (2) ALFWorld — household task benchmark, with DEPS significantly outperforming baselines. (3) Tabletop manipulation — physical reasoning tasks. Baselines: ReAct, SayCan, Code-as-Policies, and other LLM-agent approaches. Key result: near-doubled overall success rates vs. baselines on Minecraft. Voyager evaluated on: Minecraft with metrics of: unique items collected (3.3x vs. baselines), distance traveled (2.3x), tech tree milestone unlocking speed (15.3x faster than prior SOTA), and zero-shot generalization (transferring skill library to new, unseen Minecraft worlds). Baselines: ReAct, DEPS, AutoGPT, Code-as-Policies. Key result: Voyager significantly outperformed all baselines and demonstrated genuine lifelong learning — performance improved over extended play as the skill library grew. Models: Both primarily use GPT-4. Limitations: (a) evaluation limited to Minecraft — generalization to other domains not demonstrated; (b) no comparison with human Minecraft performance; (c) the skill library's growth curve and long-term saturation behavior not fully characterized; (d) no evaluation of skill quality degradation over time (do early skills become obsolete as better skills are learned?).


**SW/LLM Applicability**

- **Mapping Strength**:
  

> Strong mapping to critical gaps in JORDAN. Voyager's Skill Library maps directly to JORDAN's missing skill/memory layer — JORDAN needs a persistent, retrievable store of proven plan patterns, code solutions, and problem-solving strategies. DEPS's Describe-Explain-Plan-Select loop maps to JORDAN's Replan node — JORDAN's replanning should include structured error diagnosis (Explain) and feasibility-based sub-goal selection (Select). What transfers directly: (a) the skill library concept — store working solutions indexed by task description embedding; (b) the error-driven replanning cycle — diagnose failures before replanning; (c) the curriculum learning pattern — progressively harder tasks building on simpler ones; (d) the code refinement loop — generate, execute, self-verify, refine, commit. What needs translation: JORDAN's tasks are software engineering (not Minecraft), so the action space is different (git operations, code editing, test execution rather than Minecraft actions). The skill library for JORDAN would store: reusable plan templates, code patterns, debugging strategies, and research methodologies.

- **Gaps**:
  

> Key gaps for JORDAN: (1) Minecraft-specific action space — both DEPS and Voyager are deeply tied to Minecraft's mechanics; the concepts transfer but the implementations do not. (2) No multi-agent coordination — both are single-agent; JORDAN is multi-agent. The skill library for a multi-agent system needs shared ownership, conflict resolution, and agent-specific skill scoping. (3) GPT-4 dependence — both approaches rely on GPT-4's code generation and reasoning capabilities; performance with other models is not established. (4) No structured plan representation — DEPS plans are sequences of sub-goals in natural language, not structured DAGs with dependencies. (5) The skill library grows unboundedly — there is no mechanism for skill retirement, deduplication, or compression; over time, the library may become bloated with obsolete or redundant skills. (6) No integration with human feedback — all learning is autonomous; in JORDAN, human feedback on skill quality would be valuable. (7) Limited skill composition model — Voyager composes skills by calling them sequentially; there is no formal composition framework (parallel composition, conditional composition, resource-sharing composition). (8) The Explain phase relies on the LLM's diagnostic capability — for software engineering errors (build failures, test failures), the LLM's diagnosis may be less reliable than for Minecraft errors. (9) No explicit handling of skill conflicts — two skills in the library may make incompatible assumptions about world state. (10) The curriculum model assumes a natural progression (Minecraft tech tree); JORDAN tasks may not have a clear difficulty progression.

- **Adaptation Required**:
  

> For JORDAN: (1) Build a JORDAN Skill Library — a persistent, embedding-indexed store of: (a) successful plan templates (sub-goal DAGs for common mission types); (b) code patterns and solutions (reusable implementations); (c) debugging/error-recovery strategies (what worked when X failed); (d) research methodologies (effective search and analysis patterns). The skill library is populated from successful mission phases and queried by the Plan and Execute nodes. (2) Implement DEPS-style error-driven replanning in the Replan node — replace JORDAN's current implicit replanning with: Describe (synthesize what happened), Explain (root-cause analysis of failures), Plan (generate revised sub-goal DAG), Select (prioritize the most feasible recovery path). (3) Add a self-verification step to JORDAN's worker agents — before submitting output, each worker agent verifies its work against success criteria (tests pass, lint passes, meets spec) and iteratively refines until verified. (4) Implement a Curriculum-based mission difficulty system — progressively harder missions are assigned based on the agent's demonstrated capability level, building toward complex missions incrementally rather than attempting everything at once. (5) Add skill library maintenance — periodic deduplication (merge similar skills), obsolescence marking (skills that no longer work with current tooling), and skill quality scoring (based on success rate when retrieved). (6) Implement cross-mission memory — lessons learned from Mission A are stored in the skill library and available for Mission B. This is the single most impactful adaptation — JORDAN's current amnesia is its biggest weakness. (7) Use embedding-based retrieval for plan selection — when the Plan node generates a new plan, first query the skill library for similar past plans and adapt rather than generate from scratch (reducing cost and improving quality). (8) Implement skill composition for complex missions — a complex mission plan is assembled by composing stored sub-plans from the skill library, with the Planner only generating novel portions.

- **Analogous Jordan Node**: replan, plan, execute

**Empirical Record**

- **Validation Context**: 

> DEPS published at NeurIPS 2023. Demonstrated on 70+ Minecraft tasks plus ALFWorld and tabletop manipulation — broad domain validation. First planning-based agent to achieve ObtainDiamond. Voyager published on arXiv (May 2023) and presented at NeurIPS 2023. Demonstrated dramatic improvements over baselines: 3.3x unique items, 2.3x distance, 15.3x tech tree speed. Skill library transfer demonstrably works — skills learned in one Minecraft world transfer to new, unseen worlds. Both papers have high citation counts and significant influence on the embodied agent and LLM agent communities. Limitations of validation: (a) Minecraft-only primary evaluation; (b) GPT-4 dependence; (c) no direct comparison between DEPS and Voyager (they were developed concurrently and use different metrics); (d) skill library long-term behavior (hundreds of skills over days of play) not fully characterized; (e) no real-world deployment validation (these are research systems in game environments). The concepts have been independently validated by subsequent work: generative agents (Park et al., 2023), skill libraries in robotics (RT-2, Code-as-Policies), and curriculum learning for LLM agents.

- **Known Failures**:
  

> Documented and observed failure modes: (1) DEPS: Explain phase misdiagnosis — the Explainer attributes failure to the wrong cause, leading to replanning that doesn't address the real problem. The agent keeps failing in the same way because it's fixing the wrong thing. (2) DEPS: Plan-Explain loop oscillation — the Planner generates plan A, it fails, Explain diagnoses reason X, Planner generates plan B (addressing X), plan B fails for reason Y, Explain diagnoses Y, Planner generates plan A again (which failed for X). The loop oscillates between incompatible diagnoses. (3) DEPS: Goal Selector myopia — the Goal Selector prioritizes short-term feasible goals at the expense of long-term progress; the agent achieves many easy sub-goals but never progresses toward the main objective. (4) Voyager: Code generation failures — GPT-4 generates JavaScript that is syntactically valid but semantically wrong (e.g., the code mines the wrong block type). The iterative refinement loop may not fix semantic errors if the self-verification doesn't catch them. (5) Voyager: Skill library pollution — a skill that worked once (due to luck or specific conditions) is committed to the library and retrieved for situations where it doesn't apply, causing failures. (6) Voyager: Curriculum misalignment — the curriculum proposes tasks that are too hard (agent repeatedly fails, wasting resources) or too easy (agent doesn't learn anything new). (7) Both: Context window exhaustion — the iterative prompting mechanism accumulates code versions, error messages, and environment states, eventually exceeding GPT-4's context window. (8) Both: Minecraft-specific overfitting — behaviors that work in Minecraft may not transfer; the approaches' strong Minecraft performance may not indicate general capability. (9) Voyager: Skill interference — a new skill modifies the world in a way that breaks an old skill's assumptions, but the old skill is still retrieved and used, causing failures. (10) Both: High computational cost — GPT-4-based iterative refinement is expensive; for simple tasks, the overhead exceeds the benefit.

- **Where Still Used**: 

> Primarily research contexts with growing practical influence: (a) Embodied AI research — Voyager and DEPS are standard baselines for new Minecraft agent research; (b) Lifelong learning research — Voyager's skill library concept is studied as a model for continual learning in LLM agents; (c) Code generation — the iterative refinement loop (generate -> execute -> debug -> refine) is widely used in code-generation agents (OpenAI Codex, SWE-agent, Devin); (d) Skill library concepts are being explored in software engineering agents — storing successful code patterns, bug-fix strategies, and refactoring templates for reuse; (e) NVIDIA's ongoing embodied agent research builds on Voyager's concepts (MineDojo framework); (f) The DEPS error-diagnosis-replan pattern has influenced agent architectures that prioritize understanding WHY something failed before retrying. Direct production use is limited because the systems are Minecraft-specific and GPT-4-dependent. However, the CONCEPTS — skill libraries, error-driven replanning, curriculum learning, iterative code refinement — have been absorbed into the broader LLM agent design vocabulary and are increasingly implemented in production agent systems. JORDAN's absence of these concepts (no skill library, no error diagnosis, no curriculum) represents the most significant gap between its current architecture and the state of the art.


---

### 59. Wardley Mapping {#wardley-mapping}

**Basic Identity**

- **Name**: Wardley Mapping
- **Origin Domain**: technology strategy, business strategy, competitive intelligence
- **Year Developed**: 

> 2005 at Fotango (Simon Wardley's company); refined and expanded 2008-2010 at Canonical UK; continued evolution through Wardley's blog (blog.gardeviance.org, later medium.com/wardleymaps) and community contributions; book 'Wardley Mapping, The Knowledge' by Mark Craddock published 2021

- **Progenitor**: 

> Simon Wardley — British researcher, former CEO of Fotango, and strategist at Canonical UK. Wardley developed the mapping technique from frustrated personal experience: 'I knew Sun Tzu's five factors, I knew about the importance of the landscape... but I didn't actually know how to map it.' He created the method to operationalize strategic situational awareness, heavily influenced by Sun Tzu's 'The Art of War' and the concept of topographical intelligence in business.


**Core Philosophy**

- **Driving Insight**: 

> The central insight is that everything evolves through predictable stages of commoditization — from genesis (unique, chaotic) through custom-built (differentiating) to product (competitive market) to commodity (utility). This evolution is a force of nature, like gravity — it happens whether you plan for it or not. Strategy is fundamentally about understanding WHERE each component of your value chain sits on this evolution axis and acting accordingly (build in genesis, buy at commodity, exploit at product). The map reveals: (1) what you should build vs. buy; (2) where competitors are vulnerable; (3) where the market is heading; (4) what assumptions about your business are becoming obsolete. Most strategic errors come from applying the wrong methods to a component's evolution stage (e.g., trying to standardize something still in genesis, or custom-building something that is already a commodity). The map makes situational awareness explicit and shareable — you cannot play chess without seeing the board.

- **Decision Style**: 

> deliberative and emergent — the map provides topographical intelligence that informs decisions, but decisions themselves are human judgment calls. The map reveals: which components are strategic (genesis/custom-built, high value) vs. commoditized (low margin, utility); where inertia barriers exist; where competitors are positioned; where the market is moving. Decisions emerge from reading the landscape, not from algorithmic optimization. The methodology provides a common visual language for strategic debate — multiple stakeholders (CTO, CFO, product managers) can point to the same map and argue from shared situational awareness. Consensus emerges from seeing the same landscape. The approach combines deliberative analysis (mapping the current state) with emergent decision-making (what to do about it emerges from the map's revelations).

- **Problem Class Targeted**: 

> technology strategy and business positioning problems in dynamic, evolving markets — problems where 'what should we build vs. buy, and when?' is the central question. Designed for: medium-to-large organizations navigating technology evolution; competitive strategy (where to attack, where to defend); build-vs-buy decisions; identifying commoditization and disruption threats; organizational transformation planning. Well-suited for: dynamic landscapes where components evolve at different rates; situations requiring cross-functional strategic alignment. Less suited for: purely operational problems with no strategic dimension; problems in static, unchanging environments; problems where the value chain is trivial (one or two components). The methodology assumes the problem landscape evolves — if nothing is changing, a map adds less value.


**Phase Structure**

- **Ordered Phases**: 

> The full Strategy Cycle uses an OODA-loop-inspired sequence: (1) PURPOSE — articulate the why: moral imperative, scope of the organization, and strategic direction; (2) LANDSCAPE — map the competitive environment: identify the user, their needs, the value chain of components required to meet those needs, and each component's position on the evolution axis (Genesis -> Custom -> Product -> Commodity); (3) CLIMATE — analyze the patterns and forces acting on the landscape: anticipate how components will evolve; identify competitors' positions and likely moves; apply climatic patterns (everything evolves, no one size fits all, efficiency enables innovation, past success breeds inertia); (4) DOCTRINE — apply universal strategic principles applicable regardless of landscape: focus on user needs, use a bias for data, think fast/inexpensive/restrained/elegant (FIRE), be transparent, use appropriate methods for each evolution stage; (5) LEADERSHIP — make the strategic decisions: where to attack, where to defend, what to build vs. buy, how to organize; this is the 'Decide' step that translates map reading into action. The cycle is iterative — maps are updated continuously as components evolve. This is an Observe-Orient-Decide-Act (OODA) cycle adapted for business strategy.

- **Feedback Loops**: 

> Feedback loops are explicit and modeled on the OODA loop: (1) The Landscape phase produces a map; (2) The Climate phase reads the map and anticipates evolution; (3) Leadership decisions are implemented; (4) As components evolve and competitors move, the Landscape changes, triggering re-mapping; (5) The entire Strategy Cycle is a continuous feedback loop — faster OODA cycling is a competitive advantage (you adapt faster than competitors); (6) Within the map, component movement (left to right along the evolution axis) is inherent — commoditization is a one-way ratchet that continuously reshapes the landscape; (7) The map exposes assumptions that can be tested — 'we assume this component will stay in custom-built for 3 years' becomes a monitorable hypothesis. Loops are explicit and designed — the cyclical nature of the Strategy Cycle is central to the methodology. Faster, more accurate cycles win.

- **Gates**:
  

> No formal approval gates — Wardley Mapping is a strategic intelligence tool, not a project management framework. In practice, informal checks occur at: (1) Map validity — does the map accurately represent the value chain? Stakeholders challenge component placement and missing dependencies; (2) Strategic decision — leadership must commit to the actions derived from map reading (attack here, buy there); (3) Implementation review — are the actions producing the expected landscape shifts? If not, the map is updated and new decisions are made. There is no tollgate process, no certification, and no formal gatekeeper. The methodology is deliberately anti-bureaucratic — the pain of mapping (argument, challenge, refinement) is the validation mechanism. When consensus cannot be reached on the map, it reveals a gap in shared understanding that must be resolved through discussion and evidence. The map is never 'approved' — it is continuously debated and refined.

- **Exit Criteria**: 

> No formal 'done' state — Wardley Mapping is a continuous strategic practice. A mapping exercise is 'complete enough' when: (1) The map captures the user needs, value chain, and component evolution positions with sufficient accuracy to inform strategic decisions; (2) Leadership has reached consensus on the strategic moves indicated by the map; (3) An action plan exists. However, the map must be updated as the landscape evolves — components move, competitors act, new technologies emerge. The exit criterion for a specific strategic cycle is that decisions are made and implementation is underway. But mapping itself never ends — it is ongoing situational awareness. Partial completion: a map can be useful even when incomplete — a rough map is better than no map. The methodology encourages 'good enough for the decision at hand' rather than perfection.


**Decomposition**

- **Decomposition Method**:
  

> Decomposition is value-chain-driven and user-need-anchored: (1) Start from the user and their needs (top of the value chain); (2) For each need, identify the components required to satisfy it — components are activities, practices, data, knowledge, or technology; (3) For each component, identify what sub-components it depends on — recursively decompose down the value chain; (4) Each component is positioned on the evolution axis (Genesis, Custom-Built, Product, Commodity); (5) Decomposition is anchored by user visibility — components higher in the chain are more visible to users; components lower are invisible infrastructure; (6) This is a domain-driven, dependency-driven decomposition — what does this component need to exist? — rather than a task-driven decomposition. The value chain structure reveals dependencies (Component A needs Component B, which needs Component C). The decomposition stops when: (a) a component is a commodity utility that can simply be purchased; (b) further decomposition would not change strategic decisions; (c) the component is outside the organization's scope of interest.

- **Granularity Guidance**: 

> Granularity is governed by strategic relevance: (1) A useful map typically contains 15-40 components for a single value chain; (2) Decompose until each component can be meaningfully placed on the evolution axis — if you can't decide whether a component is genesis or custom-built, it may need further decomposition; (3) Components that are at the same evolution stage and share the same strategic treatment can be grouped; (4) A component is 'leaf-level' when it can be assigned a clear evolution stage AND a clear strategic treatment (build, buy, outsource, exploit); (5) The stopping rule: decompose until the map reveals actionable strategic insights — further granularity beyond this point adds complexity without changing decisions; (6) There is no 8-80 hour rule equivalent — the map is a strategic artifact, not a work plan. Granularity is driven by the decisions the map needs to inform, not by execution planning needs.

- **Boundary Rules**: 

> Boundary rules are user-need-anchored: (1) The map's scope is defined by a specific user and their needs — everything required to satisfy those needs is in scope; (2) The vertical chain is bounded at the top by the user need and at the bottom by commodities (components so standard they are treated as utilities); (3) Components are linked by dependency relationships — if A needs B to function, B is part of A's value chain; (4) Overlap between maps is managed by scope — different maps for different users/needs; (5) MECE is not strictly enforced — the same component may appear in multiple maps because the same infrastructure can support multiple user needs; (6) The map is a model, not a specification — clean boundaries are desirable but not always achievable; (7) The Inertia barrier concept provides a boundary check — components to the right of the inertia barrier face resistance to change from existing practices and sunk costs. Boundary disputes are resolved by debating whether a component is essential to meeting the user's need.


**Risk & Uncertainty**

- **Risk Assessment Mechanism**:
  

> Risk assessment is landscape-driven rather than checklist-driven: (1) Evolution risk — components in Genesis are high-risk (unpredictable, unproven); components in Commodity are low-risk (stable, standardized); (2) Inertia risk — components near inertia barriers risk being disrupted by more evolved alternatives; (3) Competitor positioning risk — components where competitors have superior positions represent vulnerability; (4) Dependency chain risk — a weak component deep in the value chain can compromise everything above it; (5) Assumption risk — strategic assumptions about evolution speed and competitor moves are explicit on the map and can be challenged; (6) The map reveals risk by making the landscape visible — risks that are invisible in spreadsheets or reports become obvious when mapped; (7) Risk identification is pattern-matching (against known climatic patterns) and human-judgment-based — there is no formal risk scoring, FMEA, or probabilistic analysis. The primary risk mechanism is 'seeing the board' — most strategic failures come from not seeing the threat until it's too late.

- **Unknown Handling**:
  

> When encountering unknowns: (1) Mark them on the map — 'here be dragons.' Unknown components are placed with question marks, making ignorance visible; (2) Use the map to identify what specifically is unknown — is the component's existence unknown (is it in the value chain?), its evolution stage unknown (where is it?), or its trajectory unknown (how fast is it moving?); (3) Probe — conduct research, talk to experts, run experiments to reduce the unknowns; (4) Use climatic patterns to anticipate — 'everything evolves' means today's custom will be tomorrow's product; (5) Default-safe — do not make irreversible strategic commitments in areas of high uncertainty; (6) The map frames unknowns as hypotheses to be tested, not as blockers to action. Unknowns at the commodity end of the chain are less concerning (you can buy multiple options) than unknowns at the genesis end (you are betting on an unproven technology). The methodology handles unknowns by making them visible and scoped, enabling risk-calibrated action.

- **Default Stance On Unknown**: 

> PERMISSIVE with caution — the methodology encourages action despite uncertainty because waiting for perfect information means being outpaced by competitors. However, the type of action matters: (1) Genesis-stage unknowns: experiment, explore, probe — small bets, not big commitments; (2) Product-stage unknowns: competitive analysis, market research — moderate investment to resolve unknowns; (3) Commodity-stage unknowns: low-risk — you can switch suppliers, so unknowns are tolerable; (4) The FIRE principle (Fast, Inexpensive, Restrained, Elegant) applies — act quickly but with limited exposure; (5) The map reduces the cost of being wrong — you know what you're uncertain about and can monitor it. The default is 'act with bounded risk, learn, and adapt' rather than 'block until certain.' This is a deliberate contrast with methodologies like DMAIC that default restrictive — Wardley Mapping acknowledges that in competitive strategy, the cost of inaction often exceeds the cost of being wrong.

- **Safety Guardrails**:
